author: @chaorrottai
text:
Maximum theoretical efficiency is:

Q = (Tmax - Tmin) / Tmax; Where T is temperature in kelvin, changing 1 deg c = changing 1 deg kelvin but 0 deg C = 273.15 kelvin.

It's the inverted equation for calculating the efficiency of a heat pump and all carnot engines are heat pumps when supplied mechanical power. It's also the maximum efficiency of energy extraction possible from a temperature gradient.

Low-TD carnot cycle engines minimize losses to the point where, like this they, can operate on extremely small differentials, however, low differentials are inneficient by nature. If you suplied a low-td stirling engine with a larg differential, you have an extremely efficient engine. In fact, if you control the supply flame and make sure you have efficient heat exchange, low-td carnot engines are basically the most efficient it is possible to make an engine that opperates off of pressure and heat.

With the candle flame, the flame gets to 760 celcius or 1033.15 Kelvin and if the air is 293.15 Kelvin, then the efficiency of a low-td stirling running on a candle flame would be close to 70%!!!

author: @justtinkering6713
text:
The big problem I see with most of these DIY alternator/generator projects is in the measuring and claims of power output.

I see the presenter measuring the open circuit voltage then measuring the current with ammeter in the same method. You cannot take these measurements separately then multiply them together and get a correct power measurement in watts. You have to take the voltage and current measurements at the same time. Or connect a load and then take the measurements. You are just fooling yourselves.

Stirling on nuclear waste pool ("hot")
Stirling activating another Stirling = cooling. And the more it's hot the more cold you get.
Stirling is prety the best for co-generation with concentrated Solar.

author: @billschas9202
text:
How about combining a solar heat collector with a sand battery and a bigger LTD Stirling?  The Stirling could then run when the sun is out, or as long as the battery has enough heat.  In colder climates, it might be possible to run the Stirling in the winter off the temperature difference between indoor and air without impacting the temperature inside too much.  Adding a Zeolite powered heat exchanger to the mix might have a benefit as well.

The idea is not to run a house on the Stirling alone, but to supplement other collectors, like solar panels & wind turbines to charge batteries.  This way, the collection system would take advantage of whatever climatic conditions are present.

Where have you seen large LTD engines - the water pumps that you mentioned?

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
The last few days have got me thinking that maybe we're using the wrong part of the parabola to focus the heat for a solar sterling generator.  Normally these things use the bowl to consentrate the sun's heat from behind on the underside of the engine, where a flame would be. But what if we use the legs instead and focus the heat on the upper side of the engine and use the capillary cooling from the ambient air temperature on the bottom.  

Or maybe it needs to be the other way round.  The cold side on top heated by the legs and the hot end on the bottom heated by the ambient, and let the cold fall and the heat rise.  Something I need to play with...

author: @willcool713
text: I've always thought that a Sterling engine heat pump, for heating and cooling, seemed a natural combination. Utilizing the temperature differential between inside and out to bring them further apart sounds like a great feedback loop, as long as the difference is great enough to begin with. I always figured there must be something wrong with my thinking, or it would've been done before. Probably doesn't scale well to the application.

replies.author: @BobWidlefish
replies.text: The difference between hot and cold has to be pretty large to drive such a mechanism.  Basically the range of temperature the device needs is bigger than you can comfortably tolerate.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@BobWidlefish 
"The difference between hot and cold has to be pretty large to drive such a mechanism.  Basically the range of temperature the device needs is bigger than you can comfortably tolerate."

You're information is misleading.

Rob measured the difference between hot and cold on that little one he has in this video. The difference was 5 degrees Celsius which is about 41 degrees Fahrenheit.

That's not a very big difference.

replies.author: @willcool713
replies.text: @BobWidlefish Still, you wouldn't use ambient temperatures. I imagine some form of freon would be used to do the heat exchange. Sterling engines would run the compressor, but like I say, it doesn't scale well.

replies.author: @willcool713
replies.text: @MushookieMan That would only apply to a closed system. Only the indoor end is closed.

replies.author: @willcool713
replies.text: @MushookieMan Well, if it were freon based, you'd definitely need something to pump the freon up to pressure on the appropriate side. I'm not thinking of it as a closed system, as if you're only getting energy from the Sterling engines. But as an automated control mechanism, it certainly seems like it could increase efficiency. I'm not trying to create some sort of perpetual motion device. But the heat pump concept dovetails wonderfully with the mechanics of the Sterling engine. I just see lots of room for optimization. I don't understand why everyone is so down on the idea. It's just a thought exercise I toy with now and again in idle moments. Sorry to offend.
